authors: Bicknell, R. D. C. et al.
title: Predatory drilling and the oldest-known evolutionary arms race in Lapworthella fasciculata
note: ~517 Ma, South Australia; >200 shells; shell-wall thickening tracks rise in perforation frequency. Oldest DOCUMENTED hard-shell arms race — not the origin of predation.

authors: WWF / Zoological Society of London (Living Planet Index)
title: Living Planet Report 2024
note: 73% average decline across 34,836 monitored vertebrate populations, 1970-2020. Average of population rates of change, NOT proportion of animals or populations lost; ~half of tracked populations stable or increasing.

authors: Ceballos, G.; Ehrlich, P. R.; Dirzo, R.
title: Biological annihilation via the ongoing sixth mass extinction signaled by vertebrate population losses and declines
note: Elevated extinction/decline rates; whether the '6th mass extinction' magnitude is reached is definition-dependent and debated.

title: Tiny vampires in ancient seas: evidence for predation via perforation in fossils from the 780-740 million-year-old Chuar Group, Grand Canyon, USA
venue: Proceedings of the Royal Society B
note: Oldest DIRECT fossil evidence for predation on eukaryotes (~740-780 Ma), >200 My before the Cambrian. Drill holes in vase-shaped microfossils, likely by vampyrellid-like predatory protists.

title: The origin of phagocytosis in Earth history
note: Phagocytosis (one cell engulfing/eating another — cellular basis of predation) likely originated ~1.2 Ga or earlier; timing and single-vs-multiple origins debated. LECA age 'early' (>1.6 Ga) vs 'late' (<1.2 Ga) unresolved.

authors: de Nooijer, S.; Holland, B. R.; Penny, D.
title: The Emergence of Predators in Early Life: There was No Garden of Eden
note: COUNTER-POSITION, and IMPORTANT: this is a simulation / plausibility argument that predation arose very early — NOT fossil proof. Cite it as a claim, not established evidence. No actual early predator is documented; the deep fossil record is silent. Do not let it force hedging.

authors: Ceballos, G.; Ehrlich, P. R.; Barnosky, A. D.; García, A.; Pringle, R. M.; Palmer, T. M.
title: Accelerated modern human-induced species losses: Entering the sixth mass extinction
note: Even with conservative background-rate assumptions, modern extinction rates are far above background — the authors argue we are entering the sixth mass extinction. Rate argument, not cumulative magnitude.

authors: Pimm, S. L. et al. (2014); De Vos, J. M. et al. (2015)
title: Current and background rates of species extinction
note: Current extinction rates estimated at ~1000x the background rate; De Vos et al. revised the background rate lower, making modern rates even more elevated. Method-dependent but robustly far above background.

authors: Wiens, J. J.; Saban, K. E.
title: Questioning the sixth mass extinction
note: COUNTER-POSITION: argues cumulative species lost so far does not reach big-five magnitude, so a sixth mass extinction is not (yet) established. NOTE the category error — compares a beginning (few centuries) to completed events (millennia); does not refute the RATE argument or documented defaunation. Cite honestly, then answer.

authors: Zanette, L. Y.; White, A. F.; Allen, M. C.; Clinchy, M.
title: Perceived predation risk reduces the number of offspring songbirds produce per year
note: Song sparrows protected from actual predation but exposed to predator-sound playback produced ~40% fewer offspring per year. Fear alone — no contact — drives the effect. Class E.

authors: Suraci, J. P.; Clinchy, M.; Dill, L. M.; Roberts, D.; Zanette, L. Y.
title: Fear of large carnivores causes a trophic cascade
note: Large-carnivore vocalisation playback reduced raccoon (mesopredator) foraging; intertidal prey rebounded (crabs, fish, polychaete worms). Non-lethal fear cascades down the food web. NOTE: reported prey are crabs/fish/worms, NOT mussels. Class E.

authors: Laundré, J. W.; Brown, J. S.; et al. (ecology / landscape of fear)
title: The landscape/ecology of fear: non-consumptive effects of predation risk
venue: Trends in Ecology & Evolution / review literature
note: Established framework: predation risk (not just predation) reshapes prey behaviour, distribution and reproduction, restructuring ecosystems. Class E as a framework.

authors: Dias, B. G.; Ressler, K. J.
title: Parental olfactory experience influences behavior and neural structure in subsequent generations
note: CONTESTED. Mice conditioned to fear acetophenone; F1/F2 showed altered odour response, neural change, and sperm methylation of the odorant-receptor gene. Experts: solid within mice, NOT established for humans; transgenerational epigenetic inheritance in mammals is disputed and unsettled. Cite as class I/contested — never E.

authors: Öhman, A.; Mineka, S. (2001); Isbell, L. A. (2006); primate snake-neuron studies
title: Evolved fear module / snake-detection: preferential threat detection in primates and humans
venue: Psychological Review / J. Human Evolution / PNAS
note: Humans and other primates detect snakes preferentially and rapidly; primate brains contain snake-responsive neurons. The behavioural finding is class E; the evolutionary-cause reading (predation pressure shaped attention/fear) is class I.
